# Foam-Agent run history: the run ledger

The MCP server records every simulation in `runs/ledger.md` as a mechanical
side effect of the run lifecycle — `resolve_case_dir` inserts a `planned`
row, `run_case` and its background counterpart `start_case`/`case_status`
(and the SLURM submit/status pair) flip it through
`running → done/debugging` and stamp the physics verdict; execution mode is
invisible in the record. Nobody does bookkeeping: not you, not the user.
This skill is about reading that record well and using the one sanctioned
write.

Two rules govern everything below:

- **Read cheap.** `runs/ledger.md` answers most questions by itself. The
  ledger is the record; `case_status` is the ONE tool that also reads LIVE
  progress — use it for rows whose Status is `running` (section 2). Open a
  case's logs only when the user asks for detail the ledger cannot answer.
- **Write only through `set_run_note`.** Never edit `ledger.md` with a file
  tool — hand-editing the ledger is off-limits for you. Every cell except
  Notes is machine-owned, and the Notes column is reserved for the HUMAN's
  hand edits; your note-writing goes through the tool too.

## 1. Read the ledger first

`runs/ledger.md` is one markdown table, newest row first, with columns
`ID | Case | Created | Solver | Mesh | Status | Result | Key result | Notes`.

- **Status is pipeline state, Result is the physics verdict** — separate
  facts. `done` + `diverged` is a run that finished and blew up; never
  present it as a good result. The complete vocabulary (the only legal
  values) is in `runs/run-states.yml` beside the ledger.
- The runs directory lives in the clone (bind-mounted into the Docker
  container), so your local file tools read the same `ledger.md` the server
  writes. Can't see the repo's `runs/`? `read_case_file` with the runs
  directory as `case_dir` and `ledger.md` as the path reads it through the
  server.
- Hide `archived` rows by default; include them only when the user asks
  ("all runs", "including archived ones").

Typical questions, all answerable without opening a single log file:

- **"List my runs"** → present the non-archived rows (ID, Case, Status,
  Result, Notes — condense to what fits the conversation).
- **"What happened to the dam break run?"** → find the row by Case
  (substring match: "dam break" ≈ `dam_break`), answer from Status +
  Result + Key result + Notes.
- **"Which runs failed?"** → rows with Result `diverged`, plus Status
  `debugging` for runs whose rescue is still in flight.

## 2. Live runs — `case_status` reads the one thing the ledger can't

The ledger is still the record, but a row whose Status is `running` has a
live answer the file cannot hold. `case_status(id)` (same zero-padded ID
vocabulary as `set_run_note`) is the one tool that also reads LIVE
progress:

- **"How is my run doing?"** → poll `case_status(id)` and answer in the
  run's own numbers: latest simulated time vs endTime, per-field
  residuals, Courant, the verdict-so-far (`incomplete` mid-run is correct,
  not a problem), and elapsed wall seconds — typed output from the
  convergence parser on the partial log, never eyeballed log tails.
- **The poll also settles finished runs.** The `case_status` call that
  observes the exit stamps the row `done`/`debugging` through the
  identical completion path a blocking run takes. Corollary: a row stuck
  `running` with no live process (server restarted mid-run, or the run
  exited unpolled) HEALS on the next `case_status` poll — the pidfile in
  the case directory carries a `(pid, starttime)` process identity, so a
  pid reused by an unrelated process after a restart is never mistaken
  for the run, and the dead run is stamped lazily through the same path.
  A `running` row that looks ancient is not a reason to hand-edit
  anything: one poll repairs the record.
- **"Stop my run"** → `stop_case(id)`: graceful first (the solver writes
  the current fields and exits cleanly when the case's controlDict has
  `runTimeModifiable true`), process-group kill when the grace window
  expires. The row is stamped like any finished run plus a note naming
  the deliberate stop — so when you later read a `done` row whose Notes
  say stopped-by-owner, present it as a deliberate stop, never as a
  crash. A run that finished on its own just before the call is stamped
  normally and reported `already_exited` — that is a normal outcome, not
  an error.

## 3. Zoom into logs only on demand

When the user wants more than the row holds ("WHY did it diverge?", "what
was the final residual?"), that is the cue to open the case's own artifacts:
`runs/<case>/log.<solver>` (the tail — errors and final residuals are at the
end), `Allrun.out` / `Allrun.err`, or `postProcessing/`. For force-coefficient
detail, `parse_force_coefficients(case_dir)` beats reading dat files: typed
Cd/Cl/Cm with tail-window statistics, and it re-stamps the row's Key result
as a side effect. Give the ledger's answer first, then dig.

## 4. Notes and archiving — through `set_run_note` only

`set_run_note` is the ONLY sanctioned skill-side ledger write. Arguments:

- `id` — from the ID column (zero-padded like `0003`; plain `3` is accepted)
- `note` — REPLACES the Notes cell (`''` clears it; omit to leave it alone).
  Because it replaces: when the user means to ADD to an existing note, read
  the current cell first and include it in the new text.
- `archive` — `true` archives, `false` unarchives, omit to leave Status
  alone.

Facts to respect:

- Archiving a run whose Result is still `pending` stamps it `abandoned`
  ("owner stopped pursuing this") — tell the user when that happens.
- Unarchiving restores Status to `done` and leaves Result as it is —
  `abandoned` stays until a new run overwrites it through the lifecycle.
  Only archived (or already-done) rows can be unarchived; anything else
  fails with the ledger untouched — unarchiving is not a way to set Status.
- Unknown ID → the tool errors and the ledger is untouched. Re-read the
  ledger and retry with the right ID.
- "Archive the old cylinder runs" → one `set_run_note(id, archive=true)`
  call per matching row; confirm the list with the user first when "old"
  is ambiguous.

## 5. Comparing runs

"Compare Cd across the cylinder runs" → collect the matching rows and put
their Key result, Result and Solver columns side by side — that is the
ledger-level answer. Key result is filled by the server when
`parse_force_coefficients` runs against a case with forceCoeffs output: a
compact `Cd=<x> Cl=<y> (tail mean)` stamp (machine-owned, like every cell
but Notes). Be honest about fidelity: the cell is a tail-window mean, not
the whole story — when the user wants detail (the series, the window,
min/max spread, the reference values behind the normalization), call
`parse_force_coefficients` on the case instead of reading dat files by
eye. A `-` in Key result means nobody has parsed that case's coefficients
yet: calling the tool back-fills the cell when forceCoeffs output exists;
a case with no such output cannot be compared on Cd at all — the foam
skill's forces reference (its `references/forces.md`) carries the v10
recipe to produce it. The Result verdict comes from the solver-log convergence
parser and is conservative by design (`converged` only when the parser
says so; anything else stamps `diverged`).

## 6. The zero-token alternative (mention when relevant)

`python scripts/runs.py` pretty-prints the same ledger deterministically,
no agent and no tokens: `--all` includes archived rows, `--status VALUE`
and `--result VALUE` filter. Suggest it when the user wants a quick check
that costs nothing. The ledger is also plain markdown — opening
`runs/ledger.md` in any editor is always legitimate for the human.

## 7. When the ledger looks wrong

A row stuck `running`? That is not ledger damage — one `case_status(id)`
poll heals it (section 2). A row whose case directory is gone? A case
directory with no row (e.g. a runs directory that predates the ledger)?
Don't fix it by hand — run the deterministic repair:

```
python scripts/ledger_check.py          # dry-run report (the default)
python scripts/ledger_check.py --fix    # repair, after a timestamped backup
```

It adopts orphan case directories as new rows (also the migration path for
pre-ledger runs directories), archives rows whose directories vanished
(never deletes), renumbers duplicate IDs, and repairs illegal Status/Result
values. Run `--fix` while the server is idle — its lockfile guards against
concurrent repairs, not against the server's own writes.
